questions.question:
Assume that "host1.mydomain.dom" has an IP address of 168.128.16.5. Which of the following options would be most appropriate as a subsequence of steps in performing the reverse lookup of 168.128.16.5? In the following options "NS" is an abbreviation of "nameserver".
A. Query a NS for the root domain and then a NS for the "dom" domains
B. Directly query a NS for "dom" and then a NS for "mydomain.dom" domains
C. Query a NS for in-addr.arpa and then a NS for 128.16.5.in-addr.arpa domains
D. Directly query a NS for 16.5.in-addr.arpa and then a NS for 0119 as
questions.answer: C. Query a NS for in-addr.arpa and then a NS for 128.16.5.in-addr.arpa domains
questions.to_find: Most appropriate sequence of steps for reverse DNS lookup of IP address 168.128.16.5

understand.plain: Reverse DNS lookup means finding the hostname given an IP address. DNS has a special domain called 'in-addr.arpa' reserved for reverse lookups. The IP address is written in reverse order (e.g., 168.128.16.5 becomes 5.16.128.168.in-addr.arpa) and resolved through the DNS hierarchy starting from the in-addr.arpa root.

keywords.explain: The process of finding the domain name associated with an IP address, as opposed to normal (forward) lookup which maps names to IPs.
A special DNS domain used for reverse IP address lookups. IP addresses are written in reverse octet order and appended with .in-addr.arpa.
keywords.example: 168.128.16.5 maps to 5.16.128.168.in-addr.arpa in the DNS tree

keywords.explain: A DNS server responsible for answering queries about a specific domain zone.
keywords.example: The root NS delegates queries to authoritative NS for .arpa, .com, etc.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 520 200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='140' height='40' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='80' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Root NS</text><line x1='80' y1='50' x2='80' y2='80' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><rect x='10' y='80' width='160' height='40' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='90' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>NS for in-addr.arpa</text><line x1='90' y1='120' x2='200' y2='150' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='140' y='150' width='200' height='40' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='240' y='175' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>NS for 128.168.in-addr.arpa</text><defs><marker id='arr' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='4' refY='4' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L8,4 L0,8 Z' fill='#7F77DD'/></marker></defs></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Reverse DNS lookup hierarchy: Root NS -> NS for in-addr.arpa -> NS for 128.168.in-addr.arpa -> final record for 5.16.128.168.in-addr.arpa

given.aim: Find the correct sequence of DNS nameserver queries to perform reverse lookup of IP 168.128.16.5

terms.meaning: The IPv4 address for which we want to find the hostname
terms.example: 168 is the first octet, 128 is second, 16 is third, 5 is fourth

terms.meaning: Special DNS zone for reverse lookups, hierarchically organized by IP octets in reverse
terms.connects: First we must query a NS for in-addr.arpa before drilling down

terms.meaning: DNS resolves names by starting at the root and progressively querying more specific nameservers
terms.connects: Determines the correct order of NS queries

given.plan: For reverse lookup of 168.128.16.5, the IP is reversed to form 5.16.128.168.in-addr.arpa. Resolution starts by querying a NS for in-addr.arpa (the reverse lookup root domain), then drilling down to a NS for 128.168.in-addr.arpa (the /8 block for 168.x.x.x), and so on.

steps.title: Understand reverse DNS structure
steps.apply: For IP 168.128.16.5, the reverse lookup name is 5.16.128.168.in-addr.arpa. The DNS hierarchy for reverse lookups is rooted at 'in-addr.arpa', not at the normal DNS root like '.com' or '.dom'.
steps.note: The IP octets are reversed because DNS hierarchy goes from most-specific (leftmost label) to least-specific, but IP addressing goes from most-significant (leftmost) to least-significant.

steps.title: Identify correct NS query sequence
steps.apply: Step 1: Query a NS for in-addr.arpa (the reverse lookup root). Step 2: That NS will delegate to NS for 128.168.in-addr.arpa (covering all 168.128.x.x addresses). Then drill further to 16.128.168.in-addr.arpa NS if needed. Option C says 'Query a NS for in-addr.arpa and then a NS for 128.16.5.in-addr.arpa' which correctly identifies starting from in-addr.arpa. Options A and B incorrectly use the forward DNS hierarchy (.dom domains). Option D skips the in-addr.arpa root.
steps.note: Option C is the only one that correctly uses the in-addr.arpa hierarchy for reverse lookups rather than the forward domain hierarchy.

solution.result: C. Query a NS for in-addr.arpa and then a NS for 128.16.5.in-addr.arpa domains

questions.formula_note: Reverse DNS lookup uses the in-addr.arpa domain with octets reversed; resolution follows the same hierarchical delegation as forward DNS but within the in-addr.arpa zone.

questions.question:
HELO and PORT, respectively, are commands from the protocols:
A. FTP and HTTP
B. HTTP and TELNET
C. SMTP and FTP
D. SMTP and HTTP
questions.to_find: Which application layer protocols the commands HELO and PORT belong to

understand.plain: Different application layer protocols have their own specific command sets. HELO is used to initiate a connection in SMTP (email sending), and PORT is used in FTP (file transfer) to specify the client's IP and port for the data connection. This question tests familiarity with protocol-specific commands.

keywords.explain: The HELO command is sent by an SMTP client to identify itself to the SMTP server at the start of an email transfer session.
keywords.example: Client sends: HELO mail.example.com to begin SMTP handshake

keywords.explain: In FTP active mode, the PORT command is sent by the client to tell the server which IP address and port to use for the data connection.
Simple Mail Transfer Protocol - used to send emails between servers. Uses commands like HELO/EHLO, MAIL FROM, RCPT TO, DATA.
keywords.example: Your email client uses SMTP to send email to the mail server

understand.visual_alt: HELO belongs to SMTP (email protocol); PORT belongs to FTP (file transfer protocol). These are distinct application-layer protocols each with their own command vocabularies.

given.aim: Identify the protocols to which HELO and PORT commands belong

terms.meaning: An SMTP greeting command used by the sending mail server to identify itself
terms.connects: Identifies HELO as belonging to SMTP

terms.meaning: An FTP command used in active mode to inform the server of the client's data transfer endpoint
terms.example: PORT h1,h2,h3,h4,p1,p2 where h1-h4 is IP and p1,p2 encode port
terms.connects: Identifies PORT as belonging to FTP

terms.meaning: Protocols at OSI Layer 7 that define specific commands for application services like email, file transfer, web browsing
terms.example: HTTP uses GET/POST, FTP uses RETR/STOR/PORT, SMTP uses HELO/MAIL/RCPT
terms.connects: Context for which commands belong to which protocol

given.plan: Recall standard commands for each protocol: SMTP uses HELO (or EHLO in ESMTP) for session initiation. FTP uses PORT for active-mode data connections. Match HELO->SMTP and PORT->FTP.

steps.title: Identify HELO command
steps.apply: HELO is the greeting command in SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol). When a mail client or server connects to an SMTP server, it sends HELO followed by its hostname to introduce itself. Modern SMTP uses EHLO (Extended HELO), but HELO is the classic command. This rules out HTTP, FTP, and TELNET for HELO.
steps.note: HTTP uses GET/POST/PUT/DELETE. TELNET uses NVT (Network Virtual Terminal) protocol, not HELO. FTP uses USER/PASS/LIST/RETR/STOR.

steps.title: Identify PORT command
steps.apply: PORT is an FTP command. In active FTP mode, the client sends PORT to specify where the server should connect to transfer data. FORMAT: PORT h1,h2,h3,h4,p1,p2 (6 numbers: 4 for IP address octets, 2 for port). This is FTP-specific. HTTP uses GET/POST, SMTP uses MAIL/RCPT/DATA, TELNET has no PORT command. Therefore: HELO -> SMTP, PORT -> FTP.
steps.note: Do not confuse FTP PORT with TCP port numbers — PORT is a specific FTP protocol command.

questions.formula_note: SMTP session commands: HELO/EHLO, MAIL FROM, RCPT TO, DATA, QUIT. FTP active mode data channel setup uses the PORT command with 6-tuple (IP + port).

questions.question:
Provide the best matching between the entries in the two columns given in the table below:

Column I:              Column II:
I.   Proxy Server      a. Firewall
II.  Kanna, DC++       b. Caching
III. Slip              c. Nat
IV.  DNS               d. PPP

A. I-a, II-d, III-c, IV-b
B. I-b, II-c, III-d, IV-a
C. I-a, II-b, III-c, IV-d
D. I-b, II-c, III-a, IV-d
questions.to_find: Best matching between network technologies/protocols (Column I) and their primary functions/associations (Column II)

understand.plain: This question asks you to match network protocols and technologies with their key characteristics or related concepts. Proxy Server is primarily known for caching. Kanna/DC++ are peer-to-peer file sharing applications that use NAT traversal. SLIP (Serial Line Internet Protocol) is an older protocol that was replaced by PPP. DNS (Domain Name System) acts like a firewall/security layer at the application level, though this pairing is debatable; here DNS is matched with Firewall in this GATE context.

keywords.explain: A server that acts as an intermediary for requests from clients. It often caches responses to speed up repeated requests and reduce bandwidth.
keywords.example: A web proxy caches popular web pages so multiple users can access them without re-downloading

keywords.explain: An early protocol for connecting computers over serial lines. It was the predecessor to PPP (Point-to-Point Protocol) for dial-up connections.
keywords.example: Early dial-up internet used SLIP before PPP became standard

keywords.explain: Direct Connect (DC++) and Kanna are peer-to-peer file sharing applications/networks. They commonly use NAT (Network Address Translation) for connectivity.
keywords.example: DC++ hubs allow users behind NAT routers to share files

understand.visual_alt: Matching table: Proxy Server <-> Caching, Kanna/DC++ <-> NAT, SLIP <-> PPP (predecessor), DNS <-> Firewall (application-level filtering)

given.aim: Match each technology in Column I to its primary function or association in Column II

terms.meaning: Intermediary server handling requests on behalf of clients, with caching as its primary benefit
terms.example: Squid proxy server caches HTTP responses

terms.meaning: P2P file sharing applications that require NAT traversal to connect peers behind routers
terms.example: DC++ uses active/passive modes to handle NAT

terms.meaning: Serial Line Internet Protocol - precursor to PPP for point-to-point serial connections
terms.example: SLIP lacks error correction and authentication; PPP improved on all these
terms.connects: SLIP -> d. PPP (they are related serial line protocols)

terms.meaning: Domain Name System - resolves hostnames to IP addresses; can also be used for security filtering (DNS firewall/RPZ)
terms.example: DNS-based firewalls block malicious domains by returning NXDOMAIN

given.plan: Match each item by its primary well-known association: Proxy->Caching, DC++/Kanna->NAT (P2P file sharing needs NAT traversal), SLIP->PPP (serial line protocol family), DNS->Firewall. This gives option B: I-b, II-c, III-d, IV-a.

steps.title: Match Proxy Server and SLIP
steps.apply: Proxy Server is best known for Caching (b) - it caches web content to serve multiple users faster. SLIP (Serial Line IP) is the predecessor of PPP (d) - both are point-to-point serial protocols, with PPP being the improved version. So I->b and III->d.
steps.note: Proxy servers also offer anonymity and access control but their primary well-known function in networking is caching.

steps.title: Match Kanna/DC++ and DNS
steps.apply: Kanna and DC++ are peer-to-peer file sharing programs. P2P applications frequently deal with NAT (c) - Network Address Translation - because users are behind home routers with private IPs. So II->c. That leaves DNS matched with Firewall (a). DNS can function as a firewall by blocking resolution of malicious domains (DNS RPZ - Response Policy Zones). So IV->a. Final matching: I-b, II-c, III-d, IV-a = Option B.
steps.note: This is a standard GATE 2008 question where the intended answers reflect the primary/most-common associations in the context of that era's networking curriculum.

questions.formula_note: Network protocol associations: Proxy Servers primarily provide caching; SLIP was superseded by PPP; P2P applications like DC++ require NAT traversal; DNS can serve as application-layer firewall via domain blocking.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:
i. Address Resolution Protocol (ARP) provides a mapping from an IP address to the corresponding hardware (link-layer) address.
ii. A single TCP segment from a sender S to a receiver R cannot carry both data from S to R and acknowledgement for a segment from R to S.
Which one of the following is CORRECT?
A. Both (i) and (ii) are TRUE
B. (i) is TRUE and (ii) is FALSE
C. (i) is FALSE and (ii) is TRUE
D. Both (i) and (ii) are FALSE
questions.answer: B. (i) is TRUE and (ii) is FALSE
questions.to_find: Which of the two statements about ARP and TCP are correct

understand.plain: Statement (i) says ARP maps IP addresses to hardware (MAC) addresses - this is exactly what ARP does. Statement (ii) claims a TCP segment cannot carry both data AND an acknowledgment at the same time - this is false because TCP uses piggybacking, where acknowledgments are carried in the same segment as data. So (i) is true and (ii) is false.

keywords.explain: A protocol used to discover the link-layer (MAC) address associated with a given IP address on a local network.
keywords.example: When host A wants to send to IP 192.168.1.5, it broadcasts an ARP request; the host with that IP replies with its MAC address

keywords.explain: In TCP, an acknowledgment (ACK) for received data can be included in the same segment that carries new data in the opposite direction, saving a separate ACK packet.
keywords.example: Host S sends data to R in a segment; R's reply to S carries both data (response) and ACK for S's segment in the same TCP segment

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 500 180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='20' width='140' height='50' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='80' y='42' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Sender S</text><text x='80' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>IP: 10.0.0.1</text><rect x='350' y='20' width='140' height='50' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='420' y='42' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Receiver R</text><text x='420' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>IP: 10.0.0.2</text><line x1='150' y1='45' x2='350' y2='45' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#ga)'/><text x='250' y='38' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>Data (S->R) + ACK (R->S)</text><text x='250' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>One TCP segment - piggybacking!</text><rect x='10' y='110' width='480' height='50' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='250' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>ARP: IP 10.0.0.2 -> MAC AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF (link-layer address)</text><defs><marker id='ga' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='4' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L8,4 L0,8 Z' fill='#1D9E75'/></marker></defs></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Diagram showing TCP piggybacking: one segment from S to R carries both data and ACK for R's previous segment. ARP maps IP address to MAC address at link layer.

given.aim: Determine the truth value of two statements about ARP and TCP piggybacking

terms.term: Statement (i): ARP maps IP to hardware address
terms.meaning: ARP (RFC 826) resolves a known IP address to its corresponding MAC (hardware/link-layer) address on a local network segment
terms.example: ARP request: 'Who has IP 192.168.1.1?' -> ARP reply: 'I have it, my MAC is 00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E'
terms.connects: Statement (i) is TRUE - this is the textbook definition of ARP

terms.term: Statement (ii): TCP segment cannot carry both data and ACK
terms.meaning: This claims piggybacking is impossible in TCP, which is incorrect. TCP's design explicitly allows the ACK flag and sequence number to acknowledge received data while the same segment also carries new data.
terms.example: In a bidirectional TCP connection, S sends data bytes 1-100 to R; R responds with a segment that has ACK=101 and also carries R's own data bytes 1-50
terms.connects: Statement (ii) is FALSE - TCP piggybacking is a well-known and commonly used feature

terms.meaning: Contains both sequence number (for data position) and acknowledgment number (for ACK), allowing a single segment to serve dual purpose
terms.example: SEQ=1, ACK=101, data payload present - this segment both sends data and acknowledges
terms.connects: The TCP header design proves (ii) is false - both fields exist simultaneously

given.plan: Evaluate each statement independently. (i) Check ARP definition - it resolves IP to MAC, so TRUE. (ii) Check whether TCP supports piggybacking - TCP headers have both SEQ and ACK fields, so a segment can carry data while also acknowledging previously received data, making (ii) FALSE.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement (i): ARP and IP-to-MAC mapping
steps.apply: ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) is defined in RFC 826 with the explicit purpose of mapping a network-layer (IP) address to a link-layer (hardware/MAC) address. A host broadcasts an ARP request asking 'Who has IP address X?' and the host with that IP responds with its MAC address. Statement (i) is exactly correct and is TRUE.
steps.note: The reverse process (MAC to IP) is done by RARP (Reverse ARP) or BOOTP/DHCP.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement (ii): TCP piggybacking
steps.apply: Statement (ii) claims a TCP segment from S to R cannot carry both data (S to R) AND an acknowledgment (for data from R to S). This is FALSE. TCP uses piggybacking: the TCP header includes both a Sequence Number field (carrying data from S to R) and an Acknowledgment Number field (acknowledging data received from R). Both fields are present in every TCP segment, so one segment absolutely can carry data in one direction while simultaneously acknowledging data from the other direction. This is standard, efficient TCP behavior.
steps.note: Piggybacking reduces the number of packets on the network. Without it, every data segment would require a separate ACK segment.

steps.title: Determine correct answer option
steps.apply: Statement (i) = TRUE, Statement (ii) = FALSE. The matching option is: '(i) is TRUE and (ii) is FALSE' = Option B.
steps.note: This is a conceptual question testing understanding of two fundamental networking concepts: ARP functionality and TCP's piggybacking mechanism.

solution.result: B. (i) is TRUE and (ii) is FALSE

questions.formula_note: ARP resolves IP-to-MAC addresses via broadcast on LAN (RFC 826). TCP piggybacking: a single TCP segment simultaneously carries data (via Sequence Number + payload) and an acknowledgment (via ACK flag + Acknowledgment Number field).
